Polar interrogation can be marked with high-tone ("[. . .] a cette condition est une courbe melodique montante accompagnant l'enonce [. . .] qui est globalement relativement haute, avec une modulation vers le haut sur la derniere syllabe" (Toure, 2004: 103)); this quote also seems to justify a positive mark for this feature, as the high-tone is not global to the utterance, not simply limited to a single constituent. However, there are also polar interrogtive particles: for explication, "korr"; and for assertion, "ba", and "kaa". However, they appear to be separate constructions ("D'autre facon de formuler l'interrogation est l'emploi de particules qui sont differentes de celles deja etudiees." (Toure, 2004: 103)).
This seems likely but is not expressly stated in the grammar.

Fortgens (1928) contains one example of a polar question, where word order and verbal morphology are the same as in declarative sentences. Since there is no polar interrogative particle, marking the polar question with intonation could be a possibility. Kotynski (1988) does not mention polar questions. There can be no conclusive coding of this feature from the limited information in the consulted literature.

Note also the following properties of interrogative clauses in Cavinena: "One striking feature of interrogative clauses is that they do not appear to have any specific interrogative intonation, or constituent order, or any obligatory marking that would distinguish these clauses from statements; there are question words but these can always be used with an indefinite sense. A polar question clause is identical to a statement clause, unless the (optional) first particle are ‘QUEST’ is used. Whether a particular clause is a statement or a polar question is retrieved from the context." (Guillaume 2008:100)

Polar interrogation can be indicated in several ways, by either of two particles : 'aa' is in essence a tag question particle, often seeking confirmation of a positive assumption, while 'bo' is more polite form which makes no assumption regarding the truth of the clause. Both particles may occur either clause-finally, or following an NP whose role is in question. If the former, then Modi notes that fusing of the subject nominalizer with 'aa', to produce a form '-ɲaa', is "the most common way to form polar interrogative clauses" (2017:391).
